text: The surgeon begins creating a flap medially and extends it laterally, initially entering the wrong plane but then adjusting to return to the preperitoneal plane.
The surgeon manipulates the transversalis fascia and posterior sheath medially to reposition them, ensuring entry into the correct preperitoneal plane.
The surgeon performs medial dissection below the arcuate line, where the posterior sheath is absent, exposing the underlying bone.
The surgeon performs lateral dissection, revealing the thin peritoneum of the patient. Care is taken to avoid tearing the delicate tissue during the procedure.

The surgeon carefully sweeps the cord structures away from the indirect hernia sac, aiming to reduce the entire sac without amputation. The hernia is visually small, allowing for meticulous manipulation of the cord structures.
The surgeon displays the tissue planes clearly, highlighting the edge of the hernia sac in the surgical field.
The surgeon prioritizes the cord structures, creating lateral space for the mesh placement. A 12x16 ProGrip mesh is prepared, with additional medial space being made, slightly crossing the midline.
The surgeon searches for a cord lipoma before placing the mesh into the flap. The assistant exchanges the scissors for a needle driver.
The surgeon carefully positions the mesh to ensure it sits flat without any bowing, folding, or clamshelling, before proceeding to close the flap.
